title: allex2901
body:
UNSW School of Mathematics and Statistics
                                    MATH2901
                             Higher Theory of Statistics
                               T UTORIAL EXERCISES

Contents
1   Randomness, Variability, Uncertainty, Probability       2

2   Elements of Probability theory                           4

3   Random variables                                        8

4   Random vectors                                          13

5   Common distributions                                    19

6   Transformations of random variables                     24

7   Introduction to statistical inference                   26

8   Estimation methods                                      28

9   Confidence intervals                                    31

10 Hypothesis testing                                       35
 1   Randomness, Variability, Uncertainty, Probability
1.1. (a) You flip a fair coin. Before it lands, what is the probability that it will show up Heads?
         Justify your answer.

     (b) After the coin lands on the table, you immediately cover it with your hand so that no one
         (including yourself) can see the result. Would it still be meaningful to say that ‘there is
         a probability 1/2 of observing Heads’? Why or why not?

         (Hint: The answer to this question is more philosophical than technical, and does not
         directly depend on the value 1/2. Rather, it invites reflection on different interpretations
         of probability and is intended to prompt you to consider what the concept of ‘probability’
         truly means.)

1.2. Suppose that, in a given population, 1 in 100 people has a certain disease. A test for this
     disease is 90% accurate, meaning that:

        • 90% of those who have the disease will test positive;
        • 10% of those who do not have the disease will also test positive (a false positive result).

     (a) Consider the case where a person is selected totally at random from the population,
         tested for the disease, and receives a positive result. They might be tempted to conclude:
         ‘I tested positive using a test that is 90% accurate, so there is a 90% chance that I have
         the disease’.

         Carefully analyse why this reasoning may be flawed. (We assume that the only infor-
         mation available about this person is that they were chosen totally at random and that
         their test result was positive; in particular, they are not someone who specifically sought
         testing due to symptoms or concerns.)

     (b) Now, imagine that this scenario has actually occurred: a specific individual – let’s say,
         Homer Simpson – has been selected and has tested positive. Can we meaningfully state
         that Homer has a probability α (for some α ∈ [0, 1] that may be determined) of having
         the disease?

1.3. A book has exactly 1,000 pages, numbered from 1 to 1,000. You pick up a copy of the book
     and, without looking, tear out one page totally at random. You then close the book and give
     it to a friend, who does not know which page was removed.

     (a) Before checking the book, what is the probability that page 42 is missing? Justify your
         answer.

     (b) Your friend flips through the book and confirms that page 500 is still there. Does this
         change the probability that page 42 is missing? Why or why not?

     (c) Imagine that your friend now carefully checks pages 501 through 1,000 and finds that
         none are missing. What probability should they now assign to page 42 being the missing
         one?


                                                2
        (d) Discuss how this problem relates to different ways of interpreting probability.

1.4. (a) A fair coin is flipped five times in a row, and each time it lands on Heads. A gambler
         believes that the next flip is more likely to be Tails to ‘balance out’ the results. Explain
         why this reasoning is incorrect.
       (b) The above misconception is often referred to as the gambler’s fallacy. It is often ob-
           served in real-world scenarios, such as gambling, financial markets, or sports predic-
           tions. Can you think of an example where people might fall into this trap?
       (c) If instead of five flips, the coin landed on Heads 100 times in a row, would you still argue
           that the next flip is equally likely to be Heads or Tails? Does your intuition change, and
           if so, why?

1.5.    (a) Consider measuring the height of a group of people. What are some sources of variabil-
            ity in those measurements? If you were to repeatedly select groups of n = 10 people
            totally at random from a population (what will later be called ‘random sampling’),
            would you expect to see the same values for their height in each of those samples?
            Why or why not?
        (b) A weather forecast states that there is a 70% chance of rain tomorrow. What does this
            mean? What does it not mean? If it does not rain tomorrow, does that mean the forecast
            was wrong? Why or why not?
        (c) A survey finds that 60% of a randomly selected sample of n persons (n ∈ N) in a given
            population supports a policy. If another random sample of size is taken, do you expect
            to get the same percentage? Why or why not? How does increasing the sample size
            affect the variability of the observed proportion?




                                                   3
 2      Elements of Probability theory
2.1. Let S be the sample space of a random experiment, and A, B, C be three events. Using
     unions, intersections and complements, find expressions defining the events:
        (a) only A occurs,
        (b) at least one event occurs,
        (c) at least two events occur,
        (d) exactly one event occurs,
        (e) exactly two events occur.
2.2. Prove Boole’s inequalities:                             !
                                                 n
                                                 [                   n
                                                                     X
                                             P          Ei       ≤         P(Ei )
                                                 i=1                 i=1

       and                                              !
                                             n
                                             \                        n
                                                                      X
                                         P         Ei        ≥1−            P(Eic ).
                                             i=1                      i=1

       (Hint: For the first inequality, proceed by induction. Show that this is true for n = 1 and
       then that this is true for n + 1 if true for n. For the second inequality, use the first one and
       the fact that the complement of a union is the intersection of the complements.)
2.3.    (a) If an event E1 is independent of itself, show that P(E1 ) is either 0 or 1.
        (b) If P(E1 ) is 0 or 1, show that E1 is independent of all other events.
       (Hint: Start from the basic definition of independence of events.)
2.4. Let E1 and E2 be independent events. Show that E1c and E2 are independent, and deduce
     that E1c and E2c are also independent.
     (Hint: Start from the basic definition of independence of events.)
2.5. Let E1 and E2 be two events such that P(E1 )×P(E2 ) ̸= 0. Show that, if P(E1 |E2 ) > P(E1 ),
     then
                                        P(E2 |E1 ) > P(E2 ).
       (Hint: Use Bayes’ first rule.)
2.6. Employment data at a large company reveal that 72% of the workers are married, that 44%
     are university graduates, and that half of the university graduates are married. What is the
     probability that a randomly chosen worker
        (a) is neither married nor a university graduate?
        (b) is married but not a university graduate?
        (c) is married or a university graduate?
       (Answer: (a) 0.06, (b) 0.5, (c) 0.94)
2.7. The following is a table of the annual promotion probabilities at a particular workplace,
     broken down by gender.

                                                         4
                                      Promoted        Not promoted      Total
                               Male    0.17              0.68          0.85
                              Female   0.03              0.12          0.15

      Is there gender bias in promotion? (i.e., is getting promoted independent of gender?)
      (Hint: compare conditional and unconditional probabilities of getting promoted.)

 2.8. A market researcher observes that four customers purchased Product A from a local store,
      six customers purchased Product B from a local store, and six customers purchased Product
      A from an online store. How many customers must have purchased Product B from the
      online store for product type and store type to be independent when a customer is selected
      at random?
      (Answer: 9)

 2.9. The probability of winning on a single toss of the dice is p (0 < p < 1). A starts, and if she
      fails, she passes the dice to B, who then attempts to win on her toss. They continue tossing
      the dice back and forth until one of them wins. What are their respective probabilities of
      winning?
      (Answer: P(A wins) = 1/(2 − p), P(B wins) = (1 − p)/(2 − p))

2.10. A plays tennis against B. During a given game, the score reaches ‘deuce’. Each player then
      needs to score two more points than the other to win the game. Assuming that each point is
      independently won by A with probability p, what is the probability they will have to play a
      total of 2n points to end the game? What is the probability that A will win the game?
                                                                                            p2
      (Answer: P(2n points are needed) = (2p(1 − p))n−1 (p2 + (1 − p)2 ), P(A wins) = 1−2p(1−p) )

2.11. Suppose that two balanced dice are tossed repeatedly and the sum of the two uppermost
      faces is determined on each toss. What is the probability that we obtain a sum of 3 before
      we obtain a sum of 7?
      (Answer: 1/4)

2.12. Suppose we have ten coins which are such that if the ith coin is flipped then heads will
      appear with probability i/10 (i = 1, 2, . . . , 10). When one of the coins is randomly selected
      and flipped, it shows heads. What is the conditional probability that it was the fifth coin?
      (Answer: 1/11)

2.13. Urn 1 contains 2 red balls and 3 black balls. Urn 2 contains 4 red balls and 5 black balls.

       (a) If an urn is randomly selected and a ball drawn at random from it, what is the probabil-
           ity that the ball is red?
       (b) Suppose a ball is drawn at random from urn 1 and placed into urn 2. If a ball is then
           drawn at random from the 10 balls in urn 2, what is the probability that it is red?
       (c) In the previous question, given that the ball drawn from urn 2 is red, what is the proba-
           bility that the ball transferred from urn 1 was black?

      (Answer: (a) 19/45, (b) 11/25, (c) 6/11)

2.14. Down’s Syndrome is a disorder that affects 1 in 270 babies that have mothers aged 35 or
      over. According to a study in ‘The New England Journal of Medicine’, the sensitivity of the

                                                 5
       new blood test for Down’s Syndrome (i.e., the probability of a positive test result, given that
      Down’s Syndrome is present) is 89%; while its specificity (i.e., the probability of a negative
      result, given that Down’s Syndrome is absent) is 75%.

       (a) What is the probability that a woman over age 35 would return positive on the new
           blood test?
       (b) A mother over age 35 returns a positive test result. What is the probability that Down’s
           Syndrome is actually present?
       (c) A mother over age 35 returns a negative test result. What is the probability that Down’s
           Syndrome is actually present?

      (Answer: (a) 0.2524, (b) 0.013, (c) 0.00054)

2.15. Police often set up drink-drive checkpoints — roadblocks where drivers are asked a few brief
      questions to allow the officer to judge whether or not the person may have been drinking. If
      the officer does not suspect a problem, drivers are released to continue. Otherwise, drivers
      are detained for a Breathalyser test that will determine whether or not they are arrested for
      being over the legal blood-alcohol limit. The police say that based on the brief initial stop,
      trained officers can make the right decision 80% of the time (i.e., 80% chance of testing
      someone who has been drinking, and 80% chance of non testing someone who hasn’t been
      drinking). Suppose the police operate a drink-drive checkpoint after 9 o’clock on a Saturday
      night, a time when national traffic experts suspect that about 12% of drivers are over the
      legal blood-alcohol limit.

       (a) You are stopped at the checkpoint and have not been drinking. What is the probability
           that you are given a Breathalyser test?
       (b) What is the probability that any given driver will be tested?
       (c) What is the probability that a driver who is tested is actually over the legal blood-
           alcohol limit?
       (d) What is the probability that a driver who is released without being tested had actually
           been over the legal blood-alcohol limit?

      (Answer: (a) 0.2, (b) 0.272, (c) 0.353, (d) 0.033)

2.16. You are imprisoned in a dungeon together with two fellow prisoners. You are informed by
      the jailer that one of you has been chosen at random to be hanged, and the other two are to
      be freed. You ask the jailer to tell you privately which of your fellow prisoners will be set
      free, claiming that there would be no harm in divulging this information, since you already
      know that at least one of them will go free.

       (a) The jailer refuses to answer the question, pointing out that if you knew which of your
           fellows were to be set free, then your own probability of being hanged would rise from
           1/3 to 1/2, since you would then be one of two prisoners. Show that, under reasonable
           assumptions, the jailer is wrong.
           (Hint: Show that P(you hanged) = P(you hanged | whatever the jailer says))



                                                 6
        (b) You convince the jailer, and he tells you which of the other two will be set free. You
           say this information to your fellow prisoners, and while the spared guy is jumping for
           joy, the other one asks you to switch your identities. Would you accept?
           (Hint: What is his probability of being hanged?)

2.17. A system has n independent components and each fails with probability p. Calculate the
      probability that the system will fail when

       (a) the components are in series, so the system fails if any of the components fail,
       (b) the components are in parallel, so the system fails only when all of the components fail.

2.18. Suppose a room contains n people. Assume that the 365 days of the year are equally likely
      to be birthdays for each person.

       (a) Calculate the probability that at least two of the people have a common birthday.
       (b) What is the probability when n = 23?
           (Answer: 0.507)
       (c) How many people must you ask in order to have at least 50% chance of finding someone
           who shares your birthday?
           (Answer: 253)

2.19. You have to select at random one alternative between two with a probability exactly equal to
      1/2. Unfortunately, all you have at your disposal is a coin that you suspect to be biased. De-
      note p the (unknown) probability of getting heads when it is flipped. Consider the following
      procedure:

      1) Flip the coin, and let R1 , either Heads or Tails, be the result.
      2) Flip the coin again, and let R2 be the result.
      3) If R1 and R2 are the same, return to step 1; otherwise proceed to step 4.
      4) If R2 is heads, choose alternative 1, otherwise choose alternative 2.

      Show that, using this strategy, either alternative will be selected with exact probability 1/2,
      regardless of the value of p ∈ (0, 1).


2.20. Show that it is not possible to weight two dice in such a way that the sum of the two numbers
      shown by these loaded dice is equally likely to take any value between 2 and 12 (inclusive).
      (Hint: Write fi = P(X = i) and gi = P(Y = i), i = 1, . . . , 6, where X is the number
      shown on the first die and Y the number shown on the second die. Then, write the events
      P(X + Y = 2), P(X + Y = 7) and P(X + Y = 12) in terms of the fi ’s and gi ’s and show
      that P(X + Y = 2) = P(X + Y = 12) = P(X + Y = 7) = 1/11 leads to a contradiction.)




                                                   7
 3   Random variables
3.1. Consider the game “rock, paper, scissors" in which two players instantaneously choose one
     of rock, paper and scissors. If both players pick the same item, they play again. If players
     make different choices, one of the two players wins (rock beats scissors, scissors beats paper,
     paper beats rock). Let X be the number of times the game is played until someone wins.
     Find the probability mass function of X, when the players are choosing amongst rock, paper
     and scissors totally at random.


3.2. Let X be the time it takes Jane to get home from UNSW. She catches a bus to Central, then
     a train to Westmead. On average, it takes her about 1 hour. The quickest she can get home
     is in 40 minutes, and 25% of the time she gets home in less than 52 minutes. Every now and
     then, there are delays and it takes as long as a couple of hours to get home.
     Draw a plausible probability density function fX (x) for this random variable X, using the
     above information. Where possible, indicate the above information on your diagram. Also,
     consider issues such as symmetry/skewness, unimodality/multimodality, etc.

3.3. An experiment consists of throwing a fair coin four times. Find the frequency function
     (probability mass function) and the cumulative distribution function of the following random
     variables:

      (a) The number of heads before the first tail.
      (b) The number of heads following the first tail.
      (c) The number of heads minus the number of tails.
      (d) The number of tails times the number of heads.


3.4. Let X be a discrete random variable whose behaviour is driven by the probability mass
     function:
                                          c
                                pX (x) = , x = 0, 1, 2, . . .
                                          x!
     for some constant c.

      (a) Determine c.
          (Answer: e−1 )
      (b) What is P(X = 2)?
          (Answer: 1/(2e))
      (c) What is P(X < 2)?
          (Answer: 2/e)
      (d) What is P(X ≥ 4)?
          (Answer: 1 − 8/(3e))
      (e) Obtain an expression for FX (x), and sketch it for small x.




                                                8
 3.5. A university lecturer never finishes their lecture before the end of the hour but always finishes
     within 2 minutes after the hour. Let X be the time that elapses between the end of the hour
     and the end of the lecture and suppose that the probability density of X is
                                                 
                                                    kx2 0 < x < 2
                                      fX (x) =
                                                    0     otherwise

     (a) What is the value of k?
         (Answer: k = 3/8)
     (b) What is the cumulative distribution function of X?
     (c) What is the probability that the lecture continues beyond the hour for between 60 and 90
         seconds? (Answer: 0.2969)

3.6. Let X be a continuous random variable with cumulative distribution function F (x) and
     density f (x) = F ′ (x). Find the probability density function of

     (a) the maximum, say X(n) , of n independent random variables, each of which with distri-
         bution function F .
         (Answer: fX(n) (x) = n (F (x))n−1 f (x))
     (b) the minimum, say X(1) , of n independent random variables, each of which with distri-
         bution function F .
         (Answer: fX(1) (x) = n (1 − F (x))n−1 f (x))

3.7. Let X be a continuous random variable with a probability density function fX (x) that is
     symmetric about some point ξ, i.e.,

                                fX (ξ − x) = fX (ξ + x) for all x ∈ R.

     Show that if E(X) exists, then E(X) = ξ.

3.8. Suppose that a point is chosen at random in the interior of a circle of radius 1. Let D be the
     distance of the selected point from the centre of the circle.

      (a) What is the density function of D?
          (Answer: fD (x) = 2x, for x ∈ [0, 1])
      (b) Find E(D) and Var(D).
          (Answer: E(D) = 2/3, Var(D) = 1/18)


3.9. Trees in a certain woodlot have basal diameter X (in metres) whose distribution admits the
     following pdf:
                                         fX (x) = 1I{0≤x≤1} .
     (Recall that 1I{•} is the indicator function, taking the value 1 if • is true, and 0 otherwise.)

      (a) Find the average basal diameter of the tree trunks.
          (Answer: 1/2)

                                                  9
        (b) Find the average basal area of the tree trunks.
           (Answer: π/12)
       (c) The trees have height 5 metres, and their timber currently fetches $30 per cubic metre
           when sold. Assuming cylindrical trunks, calculate the average value of trees in the
           woodlot.
           (Answer: 25π/2)
       (d) Over the next year, it is expected that all trees will increase their basal area by 0.1
           square metres, and their heights will increase to 6 metres. Find the average value of
           trees in the woodlot in a year’s time (according to projections).
           (Answer: 15π + 18)

3.10. The probability density function of the weight X (in kg) of packages delivered by a post
      office is
                                             70
                                 fX (x) =            for 1 < x < 70
                                            69x2
      and 0 elsewhere.

      (a) In the long-term, what is the proportion of packages whose weight exceeds 50 kg?
      (b) Determine the mean and the variance of the weight X.
      (c) If the shipping cost is $2.50 per kg, what is the average shipping cost of a package?
          What is the variance of the shipping cost?

      (Answer: (a) 0.0058, (b) E(X) = 4.31 kg, Var(X) = 51.42 kg2 , (c) E(cost) = 10.78$,
      Var(cost) = 321.4$2 )

3.11. Let X have the cumulative distribution function

                                      FX (x) = 1 − x−α ,     x ≥ 1,

      for some α > 0.

       (a) Find E(X) for those values of α for which it exists.
       (b) Find Var(X) for those values of α for which it exists.


3.12. Let X be a continuous random variable with probability density function fX (x) = 1I{1≤x≤2} .
      Show that                             
                                             1         1
                                         E        ̸=       .
                                             X       E(X)

3.13. Let f1 (x) and f2 (x) be probability density functions, and let a be a constant such that 0 ≤
      a ≤ 1. Consider the function f (x) = af1 (x) + (1 − a)f2 (x).

      a) Show that f (x) is a density function. Such a density is often referred to as a mixture of
         two densities.                          R
         (Hint: Show that f (x) ≥ 0 for all x and f (x) dx = 1)

                                                10
       b) Suppose that Xi (i = 1, 2) is a random variable with density fi (x), and that E(Xi ) = µi
         and Var(Xi ) = σi2 . Assume that X is random variable whose density is a mixture of the
         densities corresponding to X1 and X2 .
           i) Show that E(X) = aµ1 + (1 − a)µ2 ;
          ii) Show that Var(X) = aσ12 + (1 − a)σ22 + a(1 − a)(µ1 − µ2 )2 .
                                R             R
         (Hint: write explicitly xf (x) dx and x2 f (x) dx)

3.14. In 1994 Australia eliminated 1 and 2 cent coins, and thereafter it was intended that all trans-
      actions be rounded to the nearest 5 cents.
      Let X be the additional amount (in cents) that a shopkeeper makes from rounding transac-
      tions to the nearest 5 cents. Assuming that the number of cents for transactions is uniformly
      distributed, X has probability mass function:
                                         1
                                          5
                                              x ∈ SX = {−2, −1, 0, 1, 2}
                               pX (x) =
                                          0 otherwise
       (a) A shopkeeper wants to know if he has been losing money, because of the change to the
           5 cents system. What would you tell him? Include E(X) in your answer.
       (b) Amounts to be paid are rounded to the nearest 5 cents when paid for in cash. If paid
           for electronically (via credit card or EFTPOS), the exact amount is charged.
           You can abuse the rounding system by paying for transactions in cash when the amount
           will be rounded down, and paying electronically when the amount would have been
           rounded up. Let Y be the amount of money you save by using this approach, in a
           transaction that would be rounded to the nearest 5 cents if paid for in cash.
              i. Identify the distribution Y .
             ii. What is the expected amount of money you expect to gain per transaction by adopt-
                 ing this approach?
                 (Answer: E(Y ) = 0.6 cents)

3.15. You roll a conventional die repeatedly. If it shows 1, you must stop, but you may choose to
      stop at any prior time. Your score is the number shown by the die on the final roll. Consider
      the strategy: stop the first time that the die shows r or greater.
      a) What stopping strategy (i.e. what value of r) yields the greatest expected score?
      b) What strategy would you use if your score was the square of the final roll?
      (Answer: a) stop at the first throw showing 4 or more; b) stop at the first throw showing 5 or
      more.)

3.16. A merchant stocks a certain perishable item. She knows that on any given day she will have
      a demand for either two, three, or four of these items with probabilities 0.1, 0.4 and 0.5,
      respectively. She buys the items for $10 each and sells them for $12 each. If any are left
      at the end of the day, they are lost. How many items should the merchant stock in order to
      maximize her expected daily profit?
      (Answer: 3 items should be stocked, E(profit) = 4.80 $)


                                                 11
 3.17. Suppose that X takes on each of the values 1, 2, 3 with probability 1/3. What is the moment
      generating function? Find E(X), E(X 2 ) and E(X 3 ) by differentiating, and then compare
      with a direct derivation of these moments.
      (Answer: φX (s) = 31 (es + e2s + e3s ))
                                         
3.18. Let KX (s) = log φX (s) = log(E esX ), and show that
                                 ′                      ′′
                                KX (0) = E(X),         KX  (0) = Var(X).

      The function KX is called the cumulant generating function of the random variable X, and
      sometimes used instead of φX to represent interesting features of the distribution of X.
      (Hint: Differentiate KX (s) and use the properties of φX (s))

3.19. A type of bacteria cell divides at a constant rate λ over time (that is, the probability that a
      cell divides in a small interval of time t is approximately λt). Given that a population starts
      out at time zero with k cells of this bacteria and that cell divisions are independent of one
      another, the size of the population at time t, say X(t), has the probability distribution
                                            
                                      n − 1 −λkt
                   P(X(t) = n) =              e     (1 − e−λt )n−k ,    n = k, k + 1, . . .
                                      k−1

      a) Find the expected value of X(t) in terms of λ, k and t. Hint: +∞          i+k          1
                                                                           P            i
                                                                              i=0   i
                                                                                        x = (1−x) k+1

          for |x| < 1.
          (Answer: E(X(t)) = keλt )
      b) If, for a type of bacteria cell, λ = 0.1 per second and the population starts out with two
         cells at time zero, find the expected value of the population after one minute.
         (Answer: 806.9 bacterias)




                                                 12
 4   Random vectors
4.1. Let (X1 , X2 ) be a discrete random vector where X1 and X2 each take on either the value 1
     or −1, with E(X1 ) = E(X2 ) = 0. Show that

     a) pX1 X2 (1, 1) = pX1 X2 (−1, −1);
        (Hint: what is E(X1 + X2 )?)
     b) pX1 X2 (1, −1) = pX1 X2 (−1, 1)
        (Hint: what is E(X1 − X2 )?)

     Let p = 2pX1 X2 (1, 1). Find

     c) Var(X1 );
        (Answer: 1 )
     d) Var(X2 );
        (Answer: 1)
     e) Cov(X1 , X2 )
        (Answer: 2p − 1 )

4.2. Ascertain in the following cases whether or not F is the joint distribution function of some
     pair (X, Y ) of random variables. If your conclusion is affirmative, find the marginal cumu-
     lative distribution functions of X and Y .

     a)
                                                    1 − e−x1 −x2 if x1 , x2 ≥ 0
                                                
                               F (x1 , x2 ) =
                                                    0            otherwise
     b)                                  
                                          1 − e−x1 − x1 e−x2 if 0 ≤ x1 ≤ x2
                          F (x1 , x2 ) =   1 − e−x2 − x2 e−x2 if 0 ≤ x2 ≤ x1
                                           0                  otherwise
                                         

                                                                        2
     (Hint: Show that the second-order mixed partial derivative ∂x∂1 ∂x
                                                                     F
                                                                        2
                                                                          satisfies the conditions to
     be a joint probability density function.)

4.3. A bivariate discrete random vector (X1 , X2 ) has joint probability mass function as follows:

                                                        X2
                                                   -1    0   1
                                                0 0.01 0.08 0.01
                                       X1       1 0.05 0.42 0.03
                                                2 0.04 0.30 0.06

      (a) Determine pX1 and pX2 .

 5   Common distributions
5.1. We toss n coins, and each one shows heads with probability π ∈ (0, 1), independently of
     each of the others. Each coin which shows heads is tossed again. What is the probability
     mass function of the number of heads resulting from the second round of tosses?
     (Answer: -)

5.2. Calculate the moment generating function of the uniform distribution on [0, 1]. Obtain E(X)
     and Var(X) by differentiating.
     (Hint: Use L’Hospital’s rule.)

5.3. An uncooked spaghetti of length 1 is cut once at random. What is the probability that the
     longer piece is more than twice the length of the shorter piece?
     (Answer: 2/3)

5.4. A total of r keys are to be put, one
                                        Pat a time, in k boxes, with each key independently being
     put in box i with probability pi ( ki=1 pi = 1). Each time a key is put in a nonempty box,
     we say that a collision occurs.
                                  PkFind the expected   number of collisions.
                                                r
     (Answer: E(X) = r − k + i=1 (1 − pi ) )

5.5. Airline passengers sometimes cancel their flights at the last minute. To maximise their profits
     by not wasting available seats, it is standard practice for airlines to overbook flights; i.e.,
     to sell slightly more tickets than there are seats on the plane, on the assumption that not
     everyone will turn up for the flight. An airline sells 93 tickets on a flight that can only take
     90 passengers. The airline believes that 5% of passengers won’t turn up for the flight.

      (a) Propose a distribution that can be used to model the number of people who turn up for
          the flight.
      (b) What’s the expected number of people that turn up expecting a seat?
          (Answer: 88.35)
      (c) What’s the chance that at least one person with a ticket cannot get onto the plane?
          (Answer: 0.1504)

5.6. Consider the binomial distribution with n trials and probability π of success on each trial.
     What is the mode of this distribution? In other words, for which value of k is P (X = k)
     maximised?
     (Hint: Consider the ratios P(X = k + 1)/P(X = k), k = 0, . . . , n − 1.)

5.7. Two teams, A and B, play a series of games. If team A has probability 0.4 of winning each
     game, is it to its advantage to play the best three out of five games or the best four out of
     seven? Assume the outcomes of successive games are independent.

5.8. A basketball player has made 80% of his free throw shots during the season. Assuming he
     maintains this form in tonight’s game, find:

      (a) the probability that he misses for the first time on his fifth attempt;
          (Answer: 0.082)
      (b) the probability that he makes his first basket on his fourth shot;
          (Answer: 0.0064)

                                                19
         (c) the probability that he makes his first basket on one of his first 3 shots;
            (Answer: 0.992)
        (d) the expected number of shots until he misses.
            (Answer: 5)
 5.9. The number of yarn breaks per shift in a commercial fibre spinning machine is a Poisson
      random variable with λ = 3. Determine the probability of not experiencing any yarn break
      in a particular shift. What is the probability of experiencing more than 3 breaks per shift?
      (Answer: 0.0498 and 0.3528)
5.10. On average, 3.4 orders come into a take-away pizza shop every hour on a particular evening.
      Answer the following questions, making additional assumptions (within reason) as necessary
      to obtain an answer.
        (a) What is the chance that there is no order for take-away pizza in a given hour?
            (Answer: 0.033)
        (b) The pizza shop requires at least 3 orders per hour to break even. What is the chance
            that they will not break even for a given hour?
            (Answer: 0.340)
        (c) What is the expected waiting time between orders?
            (Answer: 17.65 minutes)
        (d) The pizza maker needs to duck out of the shop for 20 minutes. What is the chance that
            there will be no order while she is out?
            (Answer: 0.322)
5.11. The number of eggs laid by a particular bird per mating season is a Poisson random variable
      X, with parameter λ > 0. The probability that any such egg successfully develops into a
      hatchling is π ∈ (0, 1) (the probability that it does not survive is 1 − π). Call Y the random
      variable representing the number of surviving hatchlings, and determine its distribution. As-
      sume mutual independence of the development of each egg.
      (Answer: Y ∼ P(λπ))

                                  
5.12. If X ∼ P(λ), find E X+1      .

5.13. Let T be an exponential random variable with parameter λ > 0. Let X be a discrete random
      variable defined as X = k if k ≤ T < k + 1, k = 0, 1, 2, . . .. Find the distribution of X.
      (Answer: X + 1 ∼ Geo(1 − e−λ ))

            (Hint: Φ(1) = 0.8413; Φ(2) = 0.9772)
        (b) If X ∼ N (1, 9), calculate P(X < 2), P(X > 0) and P(0 < X < 2).

5.15. Most people think that the “normal" adult body temperature is 37◦ C. That figure, based on
      a 19th century study, has been challenged. In a 1992 article in the Journal of the American
      Medical Association, researchers reported that a more accurate figure may be 36.8◦ C. Fur-
      ther, the standard deviation appears to be around 0.4◦ C. Assume a normal distribution for
      body temperature for people in a given population.

                                                  20
        (a) What fraction of people would be expected to have body temperatures above 37◦ C?
           (Hint: Φ(0.5)
